answer:
**No work in the Falsafa corpus pairs both doctrines as described.**

The second doctrine—an age-specific "highest dharma" of austerity (*tapas*) in the Kṛta age, knowledge (*jñāna*) in the Tretā, sacrifice (*yajña*) in the Dvāpara, and giving (*dāna*) alone in the Kali—is explicitly stated in **Parāśara Smṛti, Chapter 1** (verses 22–23):

> [p-11656a] The *dharmas* for men are different in the Kṛta *yuga*, the Tretā, and the Dvāpara *yuga*. They are different again in the Kali *yuga*, in accordance with the character of the age.[^1]  
> [p-afd7d9] Austerity (*tapas*) is supreme in the Kṛta *yuga*; knowledge (*jñāna*) is said to be so in the Tretā. In the Dvāpara, they call it sacrifice (*yajña*); in the Kali *yuga*, only charity (*dāna*) is supreme.[^2]

However, extensive searches across the English translations (e.g., for "dharma" with "four feet"/"legs"/"foot", "loses"/"remains", and yuga/age terms; "four feet of dharma"; age-specific declines) yielded **no matches** for the first doctrine (Dharma as a cow/bull losing one of its four feet/legs per successive age). This motif appears in classical sources like the *Mahābhārata* (Vana Parva 189.8–10; Śānti Parva 59.17) and Purāṇas (e.g., *Bhāgavata Purāṇa* 1.16.21–22), but the Falsafa corpus (37 Smṛti and classical works) does not contain it, nor any work combining both.

[^1]: Parāśara Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-11656a).
[^2]: Parāśara Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-afd7d9).
